notes: Pure onboarding. One full-strength player cell versus one weak red cell to teach the first direct connection.
Early branching pressure. One weak red falls first, then the player pivots into the second target.
First slice read with a strong player start, two cheap neutrals, and one distant red anchor.
Routing via two neutrals before the final push. This is no longer tagged as the first support-loop lesson.
First obstacle-routing phase. Two circle blockers force relay expansion before the red stronghold.
First mirrored route decision after the blocker intro. Two player starts can take opposite neutrals before collapsing the center.
Twin-front pressure. Neutral timing matters because the two reds punish slow expansion.
Asymmetric pressure. One supporting allied flank plus two contested neutrals before the stronger lower-right red.
First strong central defender. Player should avoid tunnel vision on the center until the side reds are stabilized.
Three-red pressure spread. The center neutral helps the player consolidate before the final outer push.
Wide expansion check. Three neutrals create safer pacing if claimed in order instead of rushed straight into the reds.
Central stronghold with side escorts. Encourages side trimming before the final breach.
Obstacle wave starts. Dual circles force clearer lane routing without turning the phase into a maze.
Obstacle-assisted center contest. The middle neutral becomes the safest pivot once the side blockers are read correctly.
Reinforced route-reading. Triple blockers force commitment choice instead of straight-line feeding.
Most obstacle-dense World 1 non-boss phase. Low power limit is the constraint: the player must disconnect and reconnect to advance.
Repeated-cut mastery check. Two mirrored red chains reward precise disruption and timing.
Support-loop mastery plus one strong finisher. Player opening is wider, but the late push must still be coordinated.
Pre-boss endurance. Three player starts can snowball only if neutrals are converted into a stable support web.
World 1 boss closure. Multi-stage red line with obstacle choke and late stronghold; meant to reward loop setup before the breach.
Purple Menace opens immediately. One player start faces twin reds and the first purple anchor on the far edge.
Two-front lesson. One red and one purple squeeze the same center neutral from opposite diagonals.
Mirrored starts against split-color pressure. The player stabilizes both flanks before the red-purple line collapses inward.
First explicit contested-center phase. The middle neutral is the only safe route between red and purple fronts.
Expanded center contest. Two neutrals must be claimed before either hostile color can snowball across the middle.
Purple becomes a primary force. Two player starts must choose whether to split into purple first or red first.
Mid-game pressure spike. Side neutrals keep the player alive long enough to break the purple-leaning right side.
Three-neutral routing puzzle. The coalition is still hostile overall, but the pressure timings are asymmetrical.
Broad center web. Multiple neutrals and two purple cells create a denser contested-center timing check.
First high-pressure coalition spread. Four hostiles cover both diagonals and punish passive consolidation.
Spacing check before obstacle density rises. The player uses the middle neutral to stop red and purple from meeting.
Late no-obstacle escalation. The center neutral remains critical, but purple now carries the heavier side.
Obstacle-assisted routing starts. Capsule blockers split the lane geometry before the purple cluster can snowball.
Obstacle braid around a center neutral. The player must read both blocker gaps and hostile timing together.
Heavy-routing phase. Four capsules force commitment and make overextension into purple especially costly.
World 2 low-cap constraint. Two purple cells plus dense blockers force disconnect-reconnect discipline under a tiny cap.
Pre-boss escalation begins. Mixed red/purple lanes threaten both sides while the player fights for a narrow middle.
Late coalition pressure with layered blockers. Stable neutral timing matters more than raw opening aggression.
Final pre-boss endurance. The player must turn central neutrals into a durable cross-map support web.
World 2 boss closure. Two reds and two purples hold a capsule choke around the last central breach.
World 3 opens with immediate obstacle routing. One player cell chooses the top or bottom corridor around a central capsule wall.
Twin player starts learn mirrored corridor routing around left-side blockers before red and purple can stabilize.
Single-start detour phase. Two side neutrals sit behind split corridors before the first hostile pair.
Dual-start lane braiding. Both player cells fold through one center neutral before pressure reaches the right flank.
First obstacle-wall neutral contest. Top and bottom neutrals feed into a central breach behind a vertical capsule gate.
Two-entry routing check. Center support neutral anchors a push into one red and two purple fronts.
Single-start obstacle weave. Player must route through the upper or lower neutral before the center wall opens.
Purple count starts to climb. Dual starts pressure a center neutral while upper and lower purples threaten the far side.
Obstacle-first center web. One player start must choose side neutrals or a deeper center pivot before the coalition closes.
Dense mid-world routing. Three neutrals and four capsules create the first true World 3 labyrinth pressure phase.
Single-start endurance under heavy purple presence. Early neutral timing matters more than brute-force feeding.
Dual-start escalation. Symmetric neutrals and four blockers create a broad but still readable obstacle map.
Purple-heavy routing pressure. One player start must build a stable chain through side neutrals before the center collapses.
Two-player breach around mirrored diagonal blockers. Reads as the last relatively open layout before the maze stretch.
Maze phase. A single player start must spot the one viable corridor chain through five capsules before purple snowballs.
Low-cap labyrinth constraint. One player start and five blockers force careful disconnect-reconnect discipline under a tiny cap.
Coalition endgame opens. Two player starts push through narrow alternating corridors against a red-purple spread.
Late-world obstacle gauntlet. Single-start expansion through three neutrals is mandatory before the outer purple pair overwhelms the field.
Heavy-fortress pre-boss. Six capsules carve the map into staggered lanes and punish any rushed direct line.
World 3 boss closure. Two player starts breach a six-capsule fortress holding two reds and two purples behind a central choke.
World 4 opens already at endgame pressure. One player start must read the first split obstacle pair and claim a safe side neutral before the coalition settles.
Dual-start compression. A central neutral sits behind a vertical capsule gate while red and purple already cover the far side.
Single-start hostile spread. Two near neutrals keep the opening alive, but red pressure and a far purple punish overcommit.
Two-player mirrored breach. The left pair must stabilize through one center pivot before the right-side coalition converges.
Guarded-neutral endgame opener. Three capsules create a hard center wall before the player can reach the first red-purple line.
Equal-cell survival check. Two player starts face four hostiles through a single support neutral and a center wall.
Single-start fortress weave. One viable relay chain exists through side neutrals before the hostile wedge closes.
Broad dual-start pressure. Diagonal capsule pairs create two mirrored breach routes with a late right-side collapse.
Single-start coalition web. Three neutrals and a center spine force sequencing before any direct breach is safe.
Player parity by count but not by pressure. Triple-purple support on the right punishes any slow consolidation.
Hostiles outnumber the player from the start. Side neutrals are survival tools, not bonuses.
Dual-start crossfire with a central gate. The player must convert one side neutral before the inner wall can be challenged.
Purple-heavy late-game routing. The center neutral is reachable, but only through a layered obstacle braid.
Red-dominant upper fortress. Two player starts must stay coordinated or the right-side hostile stack snowballs.
Late single-front maze. Mixed neutral staging and five capsules make route choice matter more than raw cap.
Final-world low-cap constraint. One player cell and five capsules force exact disconnect-reconnect discipline under purple-leaning pressure.
Endgame gauntlet begins. Two player starts push through alternating corridors into a full coalition spread.
Full coalition survival map. Six capsules and six hostiles demand that the player turn the center-left foothold into a stable support lattice.
Pre-boss endurance wall. Three player starts are necessary just to contest the staggered late-game fortress.
Campaign boss closure. Three separated player starts must assemble through a layered inner-ring fortress before breaching three reds and three purples.
